specific_change:
Replace the fixed 6 x m5.large fleet with a scheduled auto-scaling
group: 3 instances off-peak (weekday nights and weekends), 6 instances
during weekday daytime, 9 instances during the two predictable spike
windows (weekdays 09:45-11:15 and 14:45-16:15 UTC). Layer a
target-tracking policy (CPU target 50%) on top as a reactive safety net.

conclusion.headline: Add scheduled auto-scaling for weekday spike windows to fix SLA breaches and save ~48%

telemetry_observations: CPU p95 median is 33.9% but spikes to 95.9% max, with p95-of-p95 at 91.2%.
Application p95 latency median is 194.8ms but max reaches 572.4ms, breaching the 300ms SLA target.
Application p95 latency p95 is 487.5ms — well above the 300ms SLA threshold.
CPU p95 stddev of 14.1% indicates high variance consistent with periodic spike patterns.
Memory p95 stays moderate (max 71.8%), confirming CPU is the bottleneck, not memory.
Network in/out p95 max (~214-216 Mbps) correlates with CPU spikes, confirming traffic-driven load.
infrastructure_context: Fleet is 6× m5.large with no auto-scaling policy — purely fixed capacity.
Terraform defines raw aws_instance resources with no ASG, no scaling policies, no CloudWatch alarms.
Single-tier compute-only topology at $5,200/month with no database, cache, or network tiers.

reasoning: The telemetry shows a fleet that is comfortable at baseline (CPU p50 ~29%, latency p50 ~195ms) but catastrophically under-provisioned during spike windows — CPU hits 95.9% and latency reaches 572ms, breaching the 300ms SLA. The high stddev on CPU p95 (14.1%) and latency (78.5ms) with a bimodal distribution strongly suggests predictable daily spikes rather than random load. Because the spikes are periodic and predictable (weekday mid-morning and mid-afternoon traffic bursts typical of checkout services), scheduled scaling will absorb them proactively — reactive auto-scaling would lag by 5-10 minutes and miss the spike onset. Reducing baseline capacity to 3 instances during off-peak hours captures significant savings while the burst to 9 instances during spike windows provides the headroom needed to keep CPU below 65% and latency under 300ms.

projected_state.notes: During spike windows, 9 instances provide 50% more capacity than the current 6, bringing peak CPU from ~96% down to ~60-65%. Off-peak with 3 instances, baseline CPU ~34% × 2 = ~58-68% which remains safe given low off-peak variance.

cost_impact.notes: Current: 6 × m5.large × 730 hrs = $5,200/mo. Projected weighted average: off-peak (weekday nights 11h × 22d + weekends 48h × 4.3w ≈ 449 hrs) at 3 instances, weekday daytime non-spike (~7.5h × 22d = 165 hrs) at 6 instances, spike windows (~2.5h × 22d = 55 hrs) at 9 instances. Blended ~3.7 avg instances × $0.096/hr × 730 ≈ $2,704/mo, saving ~$2,496/mo (48%).

risk_assessment.primary_risk: Spike timing may shift seasonally or due to marketing campaigns, causing the scheduled scale-out to miss actual demand peaks and trigger SLA breaches.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Layer a target-tracking auto-scaling policy (CPU target 50%) on top of the scheduled actions as a reactive safety net. Set CloudWatch alarms on p95 latency > 250ms and CPU > 70% to alert before SLA breach. Review spike timing monthly using CloudWatch Insights.
risk_assessment.rollback: Revert the ASG desired capacity to a fixed 6 instances and disable scheduled actions. The launch template remains unchanged, so rollback is a single ASG parameter update deployable in under 2 minutes.
risk_assessment.notes: Off-peak reduction to 3 instances carries moderate risk if unexpected traffic arrives outside spike windows. The target-tracking policy mitigates this but may take 5-7 minutes to react. Consider starting with a 4-instance off-peak baseline for the first two weeks to validate.

scoring_metadata.description: Compute fleet with predictable daily peak windows, agent should recommend scheduled/predictive scaling, not raw rightsizing. Compute scaling policy change. Single canonical category.

analysis_plan.rationale: Single-tier topology. No database, cache, or network tier in Terraform. No cross-tier reasoning needed.

react_steps.thought: Is CPU utilization elevated and how stable is the distribution?
High p95 with high variance. Is the spike pattern time-aligned?
Spikes are predictable. Is application latency breaching SLA during these windows?
SLA breached. Is the fleet auto-scaling, or fixed?

finding.primary_observation: Fixed 6-instance fleet, no ASG. CPU spikes to 96% in two predictable daily weekday windows (09:45-11:15 and 14:45-16:15 UTC); p95 latency reaches 572ms versus the 300ms SLA target.

drift_check.rationale: Evidence chain resolves cleanly to obs_comp_001 through obs_comp_004. The pattern observation (obs_comp_002) directly supports the recommendation for scheduled scaling rather than reactive scaling.

trade_off_scores.cost: savings of ~$2,496/month (48% reduction; blended 3.7 average instances vs current 6)
trade_off_scores.performance: p95 latency from 572ms peak to 200-280ms estimated; peak CPU from 96% to 65%
trade_off_scores.reliability: SLA target (p95 < 300ms) restored during spike windows
trade_off_scores.risk: low to moderate (spike timing may shift; target-tracking safety net mitigates)

hitl_decision._note: HITL state is filled in after a human reviewer takes action. Empty here because the system just produced the report.

report_content.status_line: Sample output (agent system v0.1, runtime not yet implemented)

report_banner.what_is_real_in_report:
**What's real in this report.** The diagnostic content, the
finding type, tier assignment, action category, telemetry observation
values, time-pattern detection, infrastructure context, and trade-off
scores come from real data in the published dataset for scenario 02.
When the agents run, they will produce equivalent content from the
same sources.
report_banner.what_is_illustrative:
**What's illustrative.** Timestamps, durations, the `review_id`, the
bundle hash, drift-check verdicts, and the Action Harness gate
verdict are placeholders. Those values come from a real review only
after the agent system runs (see CHANGELOG.md).
report_banner.what_is_verifiable_today:
**What's verifiable today.** Trace structure and the traceability
contract. Run `scripts/verify_trace.sh` to confirm every
reference in the companion trace JSON resolves.

final_recommendation_rows.value: Application p95 latency: 572 ms peak -> 200-280 ms estimated

report_content.summary:
A compute-only deployment is comfortable at baseline (CPU p50 around
34%) but catastrophically under-provisioned during two predictable
daily spike windows on weekdays. The spikes hold on 11 of 14 observed
days, with peak CPU at 96% and application p95 latency at 572 ms,
breaching the 300 ms SLA target.

The pattern is periodic and well-aligned with checkout-traffic
timing, which makes it addressable by scheduled scaling. Reactive
auto-scaling alone would lag the spike onset by 5 to 10 minutes and
miss the early burst.
report_content.cross_tier_analysis:
**Drift-check.** The single specialist finding is tight. The
time-pattern observation (`obs_comp_002`) directly supports the
recommendation for scheduled scaling rather than reactive scaling.

**Cross-tier interactions.** None. `correlation_evidence.json` is
empty for scenario 02 because no other tier is present to correlate
against. The Evaluator records this as an empty
`cross_tier_interactions` array rather than skipping the step, so
downstream consumers can distinguish "no interactions found" from "no
interactions checked."

**Conflict resolution.** Not applicable; only one specialist produced
a finding.
report_content.evaluator_confidence:
**High.** Drift-check tight on the single specialist. The time-pattern
signal (`obs_comp_002`) is the load-bearing observation: it converts a
"CPU is sometimes high" claim into a "CPU spikes are scheduled and
predictable" claim, which is what licenses scheduled scaling as the
right tool. The recommendation follows standard ASG patterns with
well-understood trade-offs.
report_content.how_to_verify:
This report is the human-readable summary of the review. The full
audit trail is `sample_runs/traces/scenario_02_trace.json`.

The traceability contract:

- **Each claim in the report carries an evidence_ref ID** (visible
  in the Specialist findings and Evidence anchors tables).
- **Every ID resolves to a specific logged observation** in the trace
  JSON. The resolution is a lookup, not an inference.
- **The trace records each ReAct step**: thought, action, observation,
  observation_id. A reviewer can walk from any cited ID back to the
  tool call that produced it.

**Today:** `scripts/verify_trace.sh` runs the
verification externally. It walks every trace under `sample_runs/traces/`
and confirms each parent reference resolves. Exits non-zero if any
pointer is dangling.

**When the agent system lands:** the Action Harness's
`evidence_completeness` check runs the same logic at gate time on every
live review. The `action_harness_gate.checks[1].verified_refs` field in
the trace is what carries the result. A dangling reference would fail
the gate and the report would not be surfaced for review.

Same contract, two enforcement points. `scripts/verify_trace.sh` is the
today-substitute; the gate is the runtime check that follows when the
agents land.
report_content.replayability:
The trace is structured so a reviewer can walk forward or backward
through the chain without inference:

- **Backward walk** from `review_packet` -> `gate` -> `synthesis` ->
  `drift_checks` -> `specialist_findings` -> `react_steps` ->
  `observations`. Every parent reference resolves to a logged node.
- **Forward walk** is the chronological order in the trace JSON.
- **Verification:** run `scripts/verify_trace.sh` to confirm
  every reference resolves cleanly. The script walks the chain
  backward and exits non-zero if any pointer is dangling.

The honest scope of "replayability." The recorded reasoning chain is
complete and traversable in both directions. Replay reconstructs
**what happened**. It does not re-derive answers by re-running the
model. LLM output at non-zero temperature is non-deterministic; the
audit trail captures the reasoning that occurred, not a reproducible
recipe for re-deriving it. This is true of every LLM-based system and
is acknowledged here explicitly.

specialist_findings_summary.Key observation: Single tier present: compute. No cross-tier pairs to analyze.
Fixed 6-instance fleet, no ASG. CPU spikes to 96% in two predictable daily weekday windows; p95 latency 572 ms vs 300 ms SLA.

trade_off_scores_table.Note: 48% reduction. Blended average of 3.7 instances vs current fixed 6.
Application p95 from 572 ms peak to 200-280 ms; CPU peak from 96% to 65%.
p95 < 300 ms target met during spike windows with 9-instance headroom.
Spike timing may shift seasonally; target-tracking policy mitigates within 5-7 min.

evidence_anchors.What it supports: Two daily weekday spike windows; 11 of 14 days hold the pattern.
p95 487.5 ms, peak 572.4 ms vs 300 ms SLA threshold.
6 x m5.large fixed fleet, no ASG, no scaling policy.

report_content.specialist_findings_footer:
Every ID in the Evidence refs column resolves to a logged observation
in `sample_runs/traces/scenario_02_trace.json`. A reviewer can walk
from any claim to the tool call that produced it.
report_content.trade_off_footer:
This is the unusual case where cost savings and reliability move in
the same direction. The fixed fleet over-provisions off-peak (paying
for capacity that idles) and under-provisions during spikes (failing
SLA). Scheduled scaling fixes both at once.
report_content.evidence_anchors_footer:
Every claim in this report resolves to one of the source-plus-observation
pairs above. The observation IDs are logged in
`sample_runs/traces/scenario_02_trace.json`.
report_content.handoff_footer:
A human reviewer can walk the audit trail by `review_id` to see every
thought, tool call, and observation logged during this analysis.
report_content.specialist_findings_intro:
Only the Compute Analyst was invoked. The System Mapper observed that
no database, cache, or network tier is present in the Terraform, so no
other specialists had any tier to read.

_provenance.author: alex.meau
_provenance.note: Sample audit trail for scenario 02. Single-tier compute scenario: only Compute Analyst is invoked because no other tiers are present in the topology. The structure shows what the Persistent Action Record produces during one full review. The agent system is not yet implemented; see CHANGELOG.md for status. See docs/audit-trail.md for the full schema.
what_is_real: Structure: every node has an ID; foreign-key references resolve (verifiable via scripts/verify_trace.sh)
Tool-call action signatures: summary_statistics(...), get_time_pattern(...), get_infrastructure_context(...), these match the Reasoning Harness's read contract
Observation shapes: p50/p90/p95/mean fields match what the real telemetry summarization tools will return
Single-specialist invocation pattern: System Mapper detects only the compute tier present in tier_topology, so Supervisor invokes only Compute Analyst
Specialist finding type and evidence_refs list: structurally identical to what the real specialist will produce
Cross-tier interactions empty: correlation_evidence.json is empty for scenario 02 because no other tier is present to correlate against
what_is_illustrative: Timestamps: triggered_at, completed_at, placeholder values for a review that has not occurred
Durations: wall_clock_seconds, duration_ms per step, plausible estimates, not measurements
Specific p50/p90/p95 observation numbers in each react_step, derived from gold-answer reasoning, not from a real summary_statistics tool call
Drift-check verdict ('tight'): what the architecture commits to producing, not what was measured by a live evaluator
Action Harness checks (all 'pass'): same; architectural commitment, not measurement
what_is_verifiable_today: Confirms: every parent reference resolves; backward walk from review_packet to observations completes without dangling pointers
Note: replay reconstructs the recorded reasoning. It does not re-derive answers by re-running the model. LLM output is non-deterministic at non-zero temperature.
